title: The bread I bake on Saturdays
summary: A twelve-year-old bakes bread every Saturday. She started two years ago, after a Sunday her grandfather wanted fresh bread.
body:
On Saturday morning, I bake bread. I am twelve. I started two years ago.

The bread is not perfect. The top is sometimes flat. The bottom is sometimes too dark. My mother eats it. My father eats it. My little brother eats it. They do not say anything bad.

Why do I bake bread? I will tell you.

When I was ten, my grandfather was sick. He stayed at our house. He was tired. He did not eat much. But one morning he said, 'I want bread. Fresh bread.'

My mother went to the shop. The shop was closed. It was Sunday.

My mother came home. She had flour. She had water. She had salt. She did not know how to bake bread. My grandfather laughed. He said, 'I will teach you.'

He sat at the kitchen table. He told her each step. Mix the flour. Wait. Knead the dough. Wait. Put it in the oven. Wait.

I watched. I was small but I remember everything.

The bread was good. My grandfather ate two pieces. He smiled.

He passed away three weeks later.

Now I bake every Saturday. The bread is not as good as his. But the kitchen smells the same. My mother sits at the table. She closes her eyes. She says, 'It smells like Sunday.'

It is Saturday. But she means Sunday. She means him.

annotations.meaningVi: không ngon bằng / không tốt bằng (so sánh ngang bằng dạng phủ định)
tôi sẽ kể cho bạn nghe (lời mở đầu câu chuyện)

grammarNotes.title: Past simple for finished events
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng thì quá khứ đơn để kể những việc đã hoàn thành. Trong bài: 'I started two years ago.' / 'My grandfather was sick.' / 'He passed away three weeks later.' Lưu ý: 'start' → 'started', 'be' → 'was/were', 'eat' → 'ate'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use the past simple for completed events. From the story: 'I started two years ago.' / 'My grandfather was sick.' / 'He passed away three weeks later.' Note: 'start' → 'started', 'be' → 'was/were', 'eat' → 'ate'.

cloze.template:
On Saturday morning, I bake bread. I am {{b1}}. I started two years ago.

The bread is not perfect. The top is sometimes {{b2}}. The bottom is sometimes too {{b3}}. My mother eats it. My father eats it. My little brother eats it. They do not say anything bad.

Why do I bake bread? I will tell you.

When I was ten, my grandfather was {{b4}}. He stayed at our house. He was tired. He did not eat much. But one morning he said, 'I want bread. {{b5}} bread.'

I watched. I was small but I remember everything.

blanks.explanation: The top of the bread is sometimes flat — when it does not rise enough.
The bottom is sometimes too dark — too cooked.
Her grandfather was sick and stayed at the house.
'Fresh bread' — bread just made, not yesterday's.
The shop was closed because it was Sunday.
'It was Sunday' — the day the shop is closed.
Flour, water, and salt — three things to make simple bread.
'My grandfather laughed' — kindly, when he saw she did not know how.
Put it in the oven — bread is baked in an oven.
The kitchen smells the same — the smell is what carries the memory.

translationVi:
Sáng thứ Bảy, em nướng bánh mì. Em mười hai tuổi. Em bắt đầu cách đây hai năm.

Bánh mì của em không hoàn hảo. Phía trên đôi khi bị lép. Phía dưới đôi khi cháy hơi đậm. Mẹ em ăn. Bố em ăn. Em trai em ăn. Họ không chê gì cả.

Vì sao em nướng bánh mì? Em sẽ kể.

Khi em mười tuổi, ông em bị ốm. Ông ở nhà em. Ông mệt. Ông ăn ít. Nhưng một sáng nọ ông nói: 'Ông muốn ăn bánh mì. Bánh mì mới ra lò.'

Mẹ em ra cửa hàng. Cửa hàng đóng. Hôm đó là Chủ Nhật.

Mẹ em về nhà. Mẹ có bột. Có nước. Có muối. Mẹ không biết nướng bánh. Ông cười. Ông nói: 'Để ông chỉ cho.'

Ông ngồi ở bàn bếp. Ông nói từng bước. Trộn bột. Đợi. Nhồi bột. Đợi. Cho vào lò. Đợi.

Em nhìn. Em nhỏ nhưng em nhớ tất cả.

Bánh ngon. Ông ăn hai miếng. Ông cười.

Ba tuần sau, ông qua đời.

Bây giờ em nướng mỗi thứ Bảy. Bánh em làm không ngon bằng bánh ông làm. Nhưng cả căn bếp có mùi y như cũ. Mẹ em ngồi ở bàn. Mẹ nhắm mắt lại. Mẹ nói: 'Mùi như Chủ Nhật vậy.'

Hôm đó là thứ Bảy. Nhưng mẹ ý nói Chủ Nhật. Mẹ ý nói ông.

questions.prompt: How long has she been baking bread?
What is wrong with her bread sometimes?
options: The top is flat, or the bottom is too dark
questions.explanation: 'The top is sometimes flat. The bottom is sometimes too dark.'

questions.prompt: Why did her mother go to the shop that day?
questions.explanation: 'I want bread. Fresh bread.' — the grandfather's request.
'The shop was closed. It was Sunday.'

questions.prompt: What three things did her mother have at home?
questions.explanation: 'She had flour. She had water. She had salt.'

questions.prompt: Who taught the mother how to bake?
options: The grandfather, from his chair at the kitchen table
questions.explanation: 'He sat at the kitchen table. He told her each step.'

questions.prompt: How many pieces of bread did the grandfather eat?
questions.hint: Look near the end of the story.

questions.prompt: What does the mother say when the writer bakes on Saturdays now?
questions.explanation: 'She closes her eyes. She says, "It smells like Sunday."'
questions.hint: Look at the last full paragraph.

questions.prompt: Why does the writer say her mother 'means Sunday' even though it is Saturday?
options: Sunday was the day the grandfather asked for bread; the smell brings him back, not the day of the week
questions.explanation: The Sunday her grandfather wanted fresh bread is the day the mother remembers — and the smell carries it.
questions.hint: Think about which Sunday matters in the story.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer bakes bread that 'is not as good as his', but the kitchen 'smells the same'. Think of a smell, sound, or small action that brings someone back to you. What about it carries the memory — and what does not?
